title: Workbook — Living in the Truth
html:
Student Workbook

For use after class or in groups. Print this page or work in your notebook.

Remembering the Essentials

  1. What does the eighth commandment forbid, and what positive duty does it impose? (CCC 2464)
  2. Why is Jesus Christ called "the Truth" (Jn 14:6), and what does that mean for how Christians must live?
  3. What is the difference between detraction and calumny? Give a concrete example of each.
  4. What is rash judgment and why does Jesus warn against it in Matthew 7:1-5?
  5. Under what circumstances is a person obliged to keep a secret, and when must a secret be revealed?
  6. What does the Catechism teach about reparation for offenses against truth? (CCC 2487)

For Reflection and Writing

Answer each question honestly. There are no "right" answers to these personal questions.

1. Read John 8:31-32. Jesus says the truth will set you free. When have you experienced this —that telling the truth, even when it was hard, brought you freedom? Or have you experienced the opposite: being trapped by a lie you told?

 

 

 

2. The Catechism says that "the right to the communication of the truth is not unconditional" (CCC 2488). Have you ever been in a situation where telling the whole truth would have harmed someone unnecessarily? How did you navigate that tension between honesty and charity?

 

 

 

3. Think about your conversations this past week. Have you shared information about someone that they would not want shared (detraction)? Have you repeated something about another person without verifying whether it was true? What motivated you to say those things?

 

 

 

4. St. Thomas More went to his death rather than swear a false oath. Is there a truth you are afraid to defend because of what it might cost you —socially, professionally, or in relationships? What would it take for you to have the courage to stand firm?

 

 

 

5. Social media makes it easy to share, comment, and judge instantly. Have you ever posted, shared, or commented something that you later realized was false, unfair, or damaging to someone's reputation? What would it look like to use digital media as an instrument of truth rather than a weapon?

 

 

 

Practical Activities

A. Truthfulness Audit

For the next three days, keep a small journal where you note every time you told a lie, a half-truth, exaggerated, or omitted something important that should have been said. Be honest —this is just for you. At the end, reflect: What patterns do you notice? What situations tempt you most to be untruthful? Write your observations:

 

 

 

 

 

 

B. Letter of Reparation

Is there someone you have harmed with your words —through a lie, gossip, rash judgment, or calumny? Write them a letter (you don't have to send it, but you may). Acknowledge the specific harm you caused. Ask for their forgiveness. If the harm is serious and you can repair it without causing greater harm, make a plan to do so. Write the letter here or in your notebook:

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

C. Media Fast & Truth Reflection

Choose one 24-hour period this week to abstain from social media and news. During that time, notice: How much of what you normally consume is truthful, verified, and constructive? How much is gossip, speculation, or outrage-driven content? At the end, write a short reflection: What would a Christian approach to media consumption look like?

 

 

 

 

D. Be a Witness to the Truth

Choose one specific, concrete action this week to bear witness to the truth. Ideas: (1) Defend someone who is being spoken ill of behind their back —say something good about them instead. (2) Correct a falsehood you see spreading online with charity and facts. (3) Tell someone the truth in love about something they need to hear, even if it is hard. (4) If you are in a situation where everyone is lying or cheating (work, school), refuse to participate and explain why —quietly, without self-righteousness. Write your commitment:

My concrete action this week: _______________________________

 

I will do it on (day): ________________________

E. Study the Martyrs of Truth

Research one of the following martyrs who died for the truth: St. Thomas More (refused to swear a false oath), St. John the Baptist (beheaded for telling Herod the truth about his marriage), St. John Nepomucene (patron of the confessional seal; killed for refusing to reveal what the queen confessed), St. Maria Goretti (died defending her purity and the truth about human dignity). Write a paragraph: What did this saint refuse to do? What was the cost? What does their witness say to you?

 

 

 

 

 

 

Scripture to Memorize

"You will know the truth, and the truth will set you free."

— John 8:32

Prayer for This Week

Lord Jesus, you who are the Way, the Truth, and the Life,
make my heart a home for truth.
Free me from the lies I tell myself,
from the half-truths that preserve my comfort,
from the words that wound another's good name.
Give me courage to speak the truth with love,
wisdom to know when to speak and when to be silent,
and humility to repair the harm my words have caused.
Holy Spirit, Spirit of Truth, dwell in me
that I may be a faithful witness
and not a false one.
St. Thomas More, pray for us.
Amen.

Print Workbook
